original_abstract: Distributional semantic models derive computational representations of word meaning from the patterns of co-occurrence of words in text. Such models have been a success story of computational linguistics, being able to provide reliable estimates of semantic relatedness for the many semantic tasks requiring them. However, distributional models extract meaning information exclusively from text, which is an extremely impoverished basis compared to the rich perceptual sources that ground human semantic knowledge. We address the lack of perceptual grounding of distributional models by exploiting computer vision techniques that automatically identify discrete visual words in images, so that the distributional representation of a word can be extended to also encompass its co-occurrence with the visual words of images it is associated with. We propose a flexible architecture to integrate text- and image-based distributional information, and we show in a set of empirical tests that our integrated model is superior to the purely text-based approach, and it provides somewhat complementary semantic information with respect to the latter.
humanized_abstract: Distributional semantic models capture patterns of word use from text and use this information to compute vector representations of word meanings. These models have proven to be highly effective for a large number of semantic tasks that require only a measure of word similarity. A problem with these models is that they are based solely on text, and human semantic experience is richly rooted in perception. We extend the semantic representations of words with information derived from perception (vision) to build a hybrid model that has the potential to provide a more complete semantic representation of concepts. We discuss in general terms how visual word detection can be used to enrich the semantic representations of terms that are known a priori to be visually related to certain images. We then describe a model architecture that integrates all three information sources and show via a set of experiments that the hybrid model outperforms the traditional distributional semantic model and that it captures a complementary set of semantic relationships.
